title: Biochemists and biomedical scientists
description: Conduct research in the genetics, nutrition, reproduction, growth, and development of domestic farm animals. Use chemistry, microbiology, engineering, and other sciences to study the principles underlying the processing and deterioration of foods; analyze food content to determine levels of vitamins, fat, sugar, and protein; discover new food sources; research ways to make processed foods safe, palatable, and healthful; and apply food science knowledge to determine best ways to process, package, preserve, store, and distribute food. Conduct research in breeding, physiology, production, yield, and management of crops and agricultural plants or trees, shrubs, and nursery stock, their growth in soils, and control of pests; or study the chemical, physical, biological, and mineralogical composition of soils as they relate to plant or crop growth. May classify and map soils and investigate effects of alternative practices on soil and crop productivity. (Based on 40 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Analyze chromosomes found in biological specimens to aid diagnoses and treatments for genetic diseases, including recognizing abnormalities in cells.
Prepare biological specimens such as amniotic fluids, blood, or tissue for chromosome examinations and other analyses.
Conduct tests on biological materials to determine chemical content, abnormalities, or cellular composition.
Harvest cell cultures using substances and techniques that promote optimal cell growth and analysis.
Stain slides or tissue specimens using specific techniques to visualize chromosomes or details for microscopic examination.
Compile and maintain laboratory records, including those for equipment maintenance and specimen analysis results.
Select, prepare, and analyze specimens and media for cell cultures using aseptic techniques and knowledge of cell requirements.
Communicate technical information about test results to patients, physicians, or researchers as required.
Supervise, train, or direct laboratory staff in testing procedures and laboratory activities to ensure quality control.
Evaluate appropriateness of received specimens for requested tests and communicate with relevant parties.
Attend continuing education programs to maintain and enhance laboratory knowledge and skills.
Conduct research in the genetics, nutrition, reproduction, growth, and development of domestic farm animals and agricultural plants.
Analyze food content to determine levels of vitamins, fat, sugar, and protein, and discover new food sources.
Study principles underlying the processing and deterioration of foods to make processed foods safe and healthful.
Research breeding, physiology, production, yield, and management of crops, including pest control and soil characteristics.
Classify and map soils and investigate the effects of practices on soil and crop productivity.
Diagnose illnesses using examinations, tests, and reports.
Order laboratory tests, x-rays, and other diagnostic procedures.
Coordinate and direct work of nurses, medical technicians, and other health care providers.
Monitor patients' conditions and progress, reevaluating treatments as necessary.
Gather and maintain patient information and records, including medical history.
Collaborate with other professionals to discuss treatment plans and patient progress.
Analyze and evaluate patient data or test findings to diagnose mental disorders.
Prepare and submit reports or summaries to government or mental health agencies.
Conduct research to develop methodologies, instrumentation, and procedures for medical application.
Develop new or improved ways of preserving, processing, packaging, storing, and delivering foods, using knowledge of various sciences.
Develop new food items for production based on consumer feedback.
Study methods to improve aspects of foods, such as flavor, nutritional value, and convenience.
Evaluate food processing and storage operations, assisting in the development of quality assurance programs.
Check raw ingredients for maturity or stability for processing, and finished products for safety, quality, and nutritional value.
Test new products for flavor, texture, color, nutritional content, and adherence to government and industry standards.
Prepare and analyze samples to study effects of drugs, gases, pesticides, or microorganisms on cell structure and tissue, and prepare requests for proposals or statements of work.
Identify and analyze public health issues related to foodborne parasitic diseases and their impact on public policies, scientific studies, or surveys.
Conduct research to develop methodologies, instrumentation, and procedures for medical applications and analyze data to present findings.
Consult and advise physicians, educators, researchers, and officials regarding medical implications of sciences such as biology and chemistry.
Plan and direct studies to investigate human or animal diseases, preventive methods, and treatments for disease.
Inspect food processing areas to ensure compliance with sanitation, safety, quality, and waste management regulations.
Develop food standards, production specifications, safety regulations, and methods for improving animal production efficiency.
Study the chemistry of living processes and the mutations in organisms that lead to diseases, including cancer.
Evaluate effects of drugs, gases, pesticides, and microorganisms on various levels and collect and analyze biological data regarding organisms and their environment.
Study spatial configurations of molecules, analyze large molecular datasets, and conduct research on cell organization and gene expression.
Develop genetic techniques and evaluate genetic data by performing mathematical calculations and analyses for research purposes.
Supervise technical personnel, including biological technologists, and provide scientific direction for project teams.
Prepare technical reports and communicate research results through publications, presentations, or project reports.
Examine physiological characteristics of specimens using microscopy to identify and classify microorganisms or assess environmental conditions affecting health.
Conduct applied research aimed at improvements in areas such as disease testing, crop quality, and the harnessing of microbes.
Study nutritional requirements of animals and develop or test new drugs or medications intended for commercial distribution.
Teach principles of medicine and laboratory procedures to students, residents, or physicians and supervise students and research.
Supervise teams, manage laboratory activities, and ensure quality control of scientific work and compliance with safety procedures.
Collaborate with biologists and other professionals to conduct genetic analyses, and confer with IT specialists to develop computer applications for data analysis.
Develop new products and procedures for sterilization, food and pharmaceutical supply preservation, or microbial contamination detection.
Communicate research findings on various types of diseases to health practitioners, policy makers, and the public.
Educate healthcare workers, patients, and the public about infectious and communicable diseases, including transmission and prevention.
Monitor and report incidents of infectious diseases to local and state health agencies.
Keep abreast of new biochemistries, instrumentation, or software by reading scientific literature and attending professional conferences.
Research the chemical effects of substances on tissues or vital processes, including drugs, serums, hormones, or food.
Research how characteristics of plants or animals are carried through successive generations.
Perform chemical analyses of microorganism content of soils to determine microbial reactions or mineralogical relationships to plant growth.
Conduct research to determine best methods of planting, spraying, cultivating, harvesting, storing, processing, or transporting horticultural products.
Provide information or recommendations to farmers regarding ways to best use land, promote plant growth, or avoid erosion.
Investigate responses of soils to specific management practices to determine the effects of alternative practices on soil productivity.
Identify degraded or contaminated soils and develop plans to improve their chemical, biological, or physical characteristics.
Conduct experiments to develop new or improved varieties of field crops, focusing on yield, quality, or disease resistance.
